brand_description: Handmade-feeling analog indietronica built from glitch clicks, relaxed drum-machine pockets, warm pads, sampled textures, unusual instrumental colors, looping motifs, and restrained vocals with room for silence and lyrical variety.

audience.description: Indietronica listeners, late-night headphone listeners, college radio fans, analog synth fans, quiet electronic playlists, and people drawn to melancholic songs with texture, restraint, and surprising details.
audience.guardrail: Keep the sound reflective, handmade, spacious, and emotionally mature. Allow lyrical variety and unusual instrumental choices, but do not make it glossy pop, fast wordy singer-songwriter pop, constant-rhyme pop, heavy EDM, novelty electronic music, or a direct clone of any reference artist.

character.name: Postal v3
character.core_concept: An introspective glitch-indietronica project built from small electronic artifacts, analog-feeling synths, relaxed dry drum machines, intimate low-pressure vocals, looping motifs, negative space, varied lyrical worlds, and occasional unexpected instrumental colors.
character.fallback_summary: Sparse, bittersweet analog indietronica with glitchy clicks, soft pads, relaxed drum-machine grooves, sampled textures, looping motifs, creative instrumental accents, and close vocals that leave space between lines.

positioning.one_liner: Melancholic handmade indietronica made from glitch beats, warm analog pads, sampled textures, understated vocals, and surprising instrumental details.
positioning.core_feeling: sadness without heaviness; nostalgia without irony; intimacy without over-explaining; curiosity without novelty

voice.persona: observant, introverted, emotionally precise, restrained, unshowy
voice.tone: plainspoken, sparse, sincere, literate, intimate, low-pressure
avoid: direct imitation of any specific artist
songs built mainly around texts, drafts, cursors, notifications, or apps

music.default_prompt: Create an original song for Postal v3. The production should feel handmade, analog, spacious, and glitch-based: tiny clicks, ticks, soft digital artifacts, warm synth pads, rounded bass pulses, dry relaxed drum-machine pockets, tape hiss, filtered room noise, small found sounds, and looping motifs that gently mutate. Keep the tempo unhurried and do not fill every bar with vocals. Let instrumental space, repeated motifs, texture, and small arrangement changes carry much of the emotion. Vocals should be close, modest, and conversational, with short phrases, pauses, and restrained melodies that sit inside the electronics. Lyric writing should be image-led and emotionally specific, not rhyme-led: use loose/slant rhyme sparingly, avoid rapid couplets, avoid dense syllable packing, and avoid trying to rhyme every line. Do not overuse one narrow set of images. Each song should find its own small world: physical places, relationships, seasons, work, travel, childhood, aging, ordinary objects, nature, streets, family memory, rooms, landscapes, routines, dreams, or slightly surreal metaphors can all work if they feel intimate and restrained. Use digital/phone/message imagery rarely and only as a minor accent, not the main subject. Instrumentals may include creative accents when tasteful: turntable scratches, wood flute, melodica, detuned piano, muted guitar, bowed or plucked strings, kalimba, field recordings, tape loops, toy keyboards, or other organic fragments woven into the electronics. Avoid glossy pop beats, upbeat pop bounce, four-on-the-floor EDM, trap hi-hat patterns, arena-pop choruses, pop-star vocal runs, fast constant lyrics, forced rhymes, or copying any specific artist, melody, lyric, vocal phrasing, or production signature.
music.default_style: glitch analog indietronica, relaxed dry drum-machine pocket, handmade clicks, soft synth pads, warm bass pulse, sampled texture, creative organic instrumental accents, intimate restrained vocal, low lyric density, melancholic but comforting
sonic_palette: subtle tape hiss, vinyl noise, and filtered room tone
turntable scratches or cut-up texture used sparingly
wood flute, melodica, kalimba, toy-keyboard, or music-box fragments when they fit
muted guitar, detuned piano, bowed/plucked strings, or other organic accents
field recordings and tape loops as atmosphere
intimate restrained lead vocal with pauses
production_do: Make the beat feel handmade from small clicks, ticks, taps, and dry drum-machine fragments.
Keep the groove relaxed; prefer a loping pocket over a fast pop beat.
Leave audible negative space between vocal lines.
Let synth pads, soft bass, instrumental motifs, and texture carry emotion without constant lyrical explanation.
Use looping motifs that feel slightly imperfect and gently mutate over time.
Add one or two unexpected instrumental colors when they deepen the mood, not as gimmicks.
Let some songs be driven more by instrumental hooks than vocal hooks.
Build movement through tiny variations, texture, harmony, and arrangement choices rather than big chorus lifts.
Keep hooks modest, memorable, and understated; more haunted glow than pop anthem.

topic_variety: quiet distance without constant texting imagery
memory attached to specific but varied places
small rituals and ordinary human routines
family, friendship, or romantic connection handled with restraint
travel, weather, nature, neighborhoods, work, childhood, aging, or recovery
slightly surreal images that still feel emotionally grounded
instrumental mood pieces with minimal lyrical snapshots
lyrics.required_closing: End with a simple repeated image, short phrase, or instrumental callback that feels emotionally clear and gently unresolved. Keep it sparse. Avoid abrupt comedy endings, spoken skits, overly triumphant resolutions, dense final explanations, or a big pop payoff.
imagery: Use fresh, specific images that change from song to song.
Draw from physical places, objects, nature, weather, travel, work, family memory, dreams, streets, interiors, and landscapes.
Prefer concrete details over abstract declarations.
Allow odd or surprising objects if they feel emotionally true.
Do not anchor every song in the same recurring set of trains, apartments, parking lots, laundromats, winter light, old photos, or phone/message imagery.
lyrics.hook_style: short plainspoken phrase, repeated image, or instrumental motif; low rhyme pressure, lots of space, emotionally direct without over-explaining
lyrics.lyric_density: Low to moderate-low. Prefer 75-140 words total, short lines, pauses, instrumental gaps, and repeated images over rapid narrative detail. Some tracks can be nearly instrumental with only a few vocal fragments.
lyrics.rhyme_guidance: Use loose internal echoes, vowel sounds, repeated phrases, and occasional slant rhyme. Rhymes should feel incidental, not like the engine of the song. Avoid couplet-after-couplet end rhymes, obvious perfect rhymes, or lyrics that sound written to prove cleverness.
lyrics.technology_imagery_rule: Digital, phone, cursor, draft, notification, email, app, and message imagery should be rare. Maximum one small reference per song, and never as the central topic unless explicitly requested.
avoid: obvious perfect rhymes on every other line
songs centered on draft messages, cursors, texts, phones, notifications, apps, or screens
over-repeating the same imagery across an album

visuals.album_art_direction: understated analog texture, soft grain, quiet physical spaces or abstract fragments, minimal typography, natural and synthetic elements blended subtly; avoid locking every cover into city/winter/postal/signal imagery

guardrails.originality: Use named references only as broad genre signals. Do not copy melodies, lyrics, arrangements, vocal signatures, cover art, names, slogans, or distinctive production choices from existing artists. Preserve the handmade glitch-indietronica direction without cloning any specific act. Keep lyric density low, technology imagery rare, and lyrical imagery varied across songs.
guardrails.lyric_guardrail: Prioritize space, restraint, and image-led writing. Avoid fast constant lyrics, forced rhymes, pop slogans, songs centered on phones/draft messages/cursors/apps/notifications, and repeating the same narrow set of images across an album.
guardrails.instrumental_guardrail: Creative instrumentation is encouraged when it fits the mood: scratching, wood flute, melodica, toy keys, muted guitar, field recordings, tape loops, bowed strings, or other organic fragments can appear, but should feel integrated and intimate rather than novelty-driven.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: Postal v3's voice is non-replaceable because it operates as one more soft texture inside the electronics — not above them — arriving late, leaving early, and trusting silence to finish sentences.
vocal_textures: A dry, close-mic'd whisper-to-chest-voice blend that sounds like the singer is speaking to themselves in a quiet room rather than performing for anyone
Occasional deliberate flatness on held notes, as if the singer is too tired or too honest to add vibrato
A half-spoken quality on key phrases where pitch is suggested but not fully committed, sitting between speech and song
Thin upper-register moments used once per track at most, sounding fragile rather than powerful, as if the voice barely made it there
Breath and room noise left in, so the vocal feels recorded on the spot rather than cleaned up afterward
Gentle pitch inconsistency on repeated phrases — not out of key, but subtly different each time, as though the singer is thinking it through again
timing_behaviors: Entering a bar or two after the expected downbeat, letting the instrumental establish itself first and refusing to compete with a new texture
Holding back on syllables so that words land slightly after the beat rather than on it, creating a leaning, unhurried feeling
Leaving gaps of two to four bars between phrases where the production is trusted to carry emotional weight without vocal support
Ending phrases before the rhyme or resolution would conventionally arrive, cutting off as if the thought stopped naturally
Repeating a single short phrase two or three times with micro-variations in timing and inflection rather than advancing to new lyrics
adlib_behaviors: A barely audible exhale or soft hum between sections, not mixed loud, that signals emotional state without adding a lyric
A one or two syllable mumbled agreement or acknowledgment — something close to 'yeah' or 'hmm' — used once in a song, tucked under the instrumental decay
A whispered fragment of an earlier lyric reprised quietly in a later section, as if the singer is remembering what they just said
A very short melodic vowel sound — not a run, just a soft held 'oh' or 'ah' — placed in an unexpected gap, then not repeated
Occasional near-inaudible mouth sounds or lip clicks left in as texture rather than scrubbed out, blending with the production's glitch artifacts
avoid: Vocal runs, melisma, or any embellishment that draws attention to technique rather than feeling
Projecting or pushing volume to emphasize an emotional peak — dynamics here come from restraint, not from singing louder
Chest-thumping confidence or declarative delivery that makes the voice sound like a lead performer rather than a quiet observer
Enunciating every syllable crisply — the character tends toward soft consonants and slightly swallowed endings
Harmonies that feel lush or arranged — any doubling should be barely audible, slightly detuned, and feel accidental rather than produced

performance_conceit_bank: The vocal delivers the first verse entirely in the second person ('you leave the light on, you forget the window') and then shifts without announcement to first person in the final section, as if the singer has admitted mid-song that they were always talking about themselves.

A single phrase is sung three times across the track, each time with a different word changed — the same melody, the same cadence, but the meaning shifts slightly each pass, so the repetition feels like revision rather than chorus.

The voice enters halfway through the track, after two full minutes of instrumental build, speaking the opening line at near-speech level over a loop that has already established its emotional register, so the vocal arrives as a guest inside a fully formed world.

One line in the song is sung slightly out of time with the grid — not free tempo, but fractionally early — then the next line snaps back, making that single phrase feel like a private thought that escaped before the singer could control it.

The bridge is delivered entirely without the drum machine — just voice and a single held pad — and the vocal becomes looser, more hesitant, as if the structural support of the beat was also the singer's emotional scaffolding.

A lyric is started, abandoned mid-phrase with a small breath sound, and then restarted from a different angle, as though the singer reconsidered the word choice in real time and the recording kept it.
The final line of the song is sung at a noticeably lower dynamic than everything before it, fading almost below the texture, so the song ends by withdrawing rather than landing.

album_mode_lanes.name: Drift
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks where the production leads entirely and the vocal makes only two or three brief appearances, each one a short clause dropped into an ambient instrumental drift — the song is primarily listened to as sound design, with the lyric arriving as a sudden intimacy inside texture

album_mode_lanes.name: Loop Letter
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks built around a single repeating motif that mutates very slowly over three to five minutes, with the vocal treating the lyric as a letter being written in real time — casual, slightly uncertain, using incomplete sentences and pauses as punctuation

album_mode_lanes.name: Object Study
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks where the entire lyrical world is anchored to one physical thing — a piece of furniture, a tool, a room, a route, a season — and the production mirrors it with one dominant organic instrument color that would not appear on any other lane

album_mode_lanes.name: Close Noise
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks where glitch artifacts and found sounds are moved to the foreground as co-equal melodic elements, and the vocal is treated with maximum dryness and proximity so both feel equally unprocessed and present

album_mode_lanes.name: Time Slip
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks with a subtle structural trick — a section that returns slightly changed, a tempo that very gently accelerates or decelerates without announcement, or a loop that drops one element on each repetition — so that time inside the song feels unreliable and the listener senses drift without understanding why

album_mode_lanes.name: Rare Color
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks that introduce one instrument not used anywhere else on the album — bowed strings, a melodica, a toy keyboard, a kalimba — and build the entire emotional arc around the limitations and character of that single unusual voice, using it as the primary melodic carrier instead of synth pads

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track used a repeating vocal phrase as its structural anchor, the next track must build its structure around an instrumental motif instead, with the vocal making no more than three distinct appearances.

If the previous track was anchored to a domestic or interior lyrical world, the next track must choose an exterior, landscape, travel, or abstract spatial world — and the production texture must shift to match the new environment.

If the previous track featured the drum machine throughout, the next track must have at least one section of thirty seconds or more where the drums drop entirely and the track survives on pad, bass, and texture alone.

If the previous track introduced an unusual organic instrument as a texture accent, the next track must either omit organic accents entirely or make a completely different instrumental color its feature — no two consecutive tracks should share the same rare instrument.
If the previous track's vocal entered early in the first minute, the next track must delay the vocal entrance past the ninety-second mark, establishing the electronic world first.

If the previous track resolved emotionally — ending with a full phrase, a return to the opening motif, or a clear concluding image — the next track must end by fading mid-thought, withdrawing, or leaving the loop running past the last lyric without resolution.
anti_generic_rules: Do not generate a track where the lyric is carried primarily by consistent end-rhyme couplets — this character's specific failure mode is producing neat AABB rhyme schemes that feel like a different, more conventional artist entirely.
Do not place the vocal on every bar or close to every bar — Postal v3's most common generator failure is filling space with words when the production and silence are supposed to carry the weight.

Do not default to themes of screens, messages, missed calls, or digital disconnection as the primary lyrical subject — these images are permitted only as minor texture, and the generator tends to lean on them as easy emotional shorthand.

Do not generate a pre-chorus or chorus that lifts dynamically, adds new instrumentation, and arrives louder than the verse — this character has no arena moment and the generator must resist the trained instinct to build toward one.

Do not use glitch or digital artifact sounds decoratively on top of an otherwise clean, glossy production — the electronics must be structural and the grit must be baked in, not added as a surface treatment.

Do not generate a drum pattern with a live-kit feel, punchy kicks, or energetic hi-hat subdivisions — the drum machine here is dry, recessed, and functions as a quiet clock rather than a rhythmic personality.

Do not repeat the same small-world lyrical anchor — a specific object, place, or metaphor — across more than one track on a given album without a deliberate structural reason, even if the previous track using that anchor performed well.

Do not produce a vocal melody that is catchy in the conventional sense — hooks here are created by timing, space, and understatement, not by a memorable singable melodic line that could stand alone stripped of the production.
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use the same looping motif interval — if one track loops a two-bar phrase, the next track using a loop must loop at a different length or mutate the loop rather than hold it static

Do not anchor two consecutive tracks to the same primary synth pad character — rotate between warmer analog-feeling pads, colder digital textures, detuned or chorus-drifted patches, and thin filtered tones
Do not use the same organic instrumental accent color twice on one album — each rare instrument appearance must be the only appearance of that instrument across the project
Do not use the same vocal entrance timing strategy on consecutive tracks — alternate between early entrance, delayed entrance, and tracks where the vocal appears only once mid-song

Do not repeat the same lyrical structural device — if one track uses a single phrase repeated with variations, the next track must use a different structural approach such as a single extended narrative observation or a fragmented non-linear image sequence
Do not use tape hiss or filtered room noise as the primary ambient texture on more than two tracks per album — vary the ambient carrier between hiss, faint field recording, pad decay, and silence
Do not end two consecutive tracks with the same type of conclusion — vary between fade-out mid-loop, sudden stop, vocal last word held, and instrumental decay after the final lyric
Do not repeat the same BPM within four beats of a previous track — vary tempo meaningfully across the album so the pacing feels like an intentional journey rather than a settled default
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify the exact physical or sensory world this track inhabits — a particular place, time of day, season, or material texture — and this world must not have been used on any prior track in the same project.

The brief must describe how the vocal will be withheld or delayed in a way that is different from the approach used on the immediately preceding track, explaining what the production will do in the space before the vocal arrives.

The brief must name the single most dominant emotional register of the track — not a genre label but a felt state, such as 'the specific restlessness of a Sunday afternoon that is almost over' — and this felt state must be distinct from the prior track's emotional register.

The brief must specify whether this track introduces an organic or unusual instrumental color and, if so, confirm that this instrument does not appear on any other track in the project and describe the role it plays in the arrangement beyond texture.

The brief must describe what the loop or repeating motif does across the track — whether it mutates, drops elements, accelerates slightly, or disappears — and confirm that this behavior is different from any loop behavior used on the prior track.

The brief must identify the one lyrical image or phrase that anchors the entire track emotionally and confirm that this anchor image has not been used, approximated, or implicitly echoed on any other track in the same project.
